Patterns of importation risk to Europe may rapidly shift under uncertainty surrounding the geographical extent and evolution of the outbreak, particularly if transmission expands into major urban hubs with strong international connectivity near the affected area.[245] The analysis does not quantify this uncertainty in probabilistic terms.

Seasonal and temporal representativeness of travel data: The analyses use cumulative passenger traffic from May, June, and July 2024, a period that showed moderate seasonal variability relative to the 2024 annual average.[243] The authors do not discuss whether outbreak-related changes in travel behaviour (e.g., reduced tourism or business travel to affected regions) would alter the estimates.

No adjustment for detection, infectious period, or per-capita incidence: The method does not incorporate incubation period distributions, probability of travelling while infectious, exit or entry screening effectiveness, or source-area incidence rates. As a consequence, all scenarios implicitly assume equal per-traveller exportation risk from each seed airport, which the authors do not explicitly flag as a limitation but is a direct consequence of the proportional-flow methodology.

Hypothetical nature of amplification scenarios: Scenarios 4–6 explore hypothetical amplification assuming community transmission in major urban hubs.[244] The analysis does not assign likelihoods to these scenarios or model the transmission dynamics required to reach that state, limiting their direct operational applicability.

Data sources¶

Primary outbreak data: As of 29 May 2026, the outbreak involved 125 confirmed cases (17 deaths) and 906 suspected cases (223 deaths) across Ituri, North Kivu, and South Kivu provinces in Eastern DRC, and 9 confirmed cases (1 death) in Uganda.[246] These figures inform the scenario definitions (which areas to include as seeds) but are not directly used as a quantitative input to the model formula.

Auxiliary data: Origin-destination passenger flows are derived from the 2024 IATA database, which captures complete passenger itineraries including connecting flights.[238] Analyses are based on cumulative passenger traffic reported for May, June, and July 2024.[247] Relative to annual traffic to Europe, mean passenger volumes during May–July ranged from 3% below to 16% above the monthly 2024 average across scenarios.[248] No line list, demographic data, or parameters borrowed from prior outbreaks are used as model inputs; the method relies solely on the IATA travel volumes and the airport-level scenario seeding definitions.

van Zandvoort et al. (2026) The risk of global Ebola virus spread is low: epidemiology of Ebola disease cases outside Africa, 1976 to May 2026¶

Read the report

Overview¶

This analysis reviews the historical record of confirmed Ebola disease cases presenting outside Africa to contextualize the intercontinental spread risk posed by the 2026 Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) outbreak in DRC. The authors conducted internet searches — both manual and AI-assisted — to identify all laboratory-confirmed Ebola disease cases presenting outside Africa, without any time limit.[249] The main finding is that intercontinental transmission risk remains low, based on the full historical record of such cases.[250]

Estimate¶

The analysis does not produce an outbreak size estimate for the 2026 BVD outbreak itself. Instead it reports importation risk metrics derived from the historical record.

The crude overall risk since 2000 is 0.81 cases outside Africa per 1,000 Ebola disease cases in a source outbreak (including medically evacuated, latent, and secondary cases); focusing on latent cases and their secondary cases only, the rate is 0.17 cases outside Africa per 1,000 source outbreak cases.[251] This represents the empirically observed risk across all countries outside Africa combined; per-country risks are smaller — for example, for the UK alone, the estimate is 0.03 latent cases per 1,000 source outbreak cases.[252]

From the time-varying analysis of the 2014–16 epidemic, exportation risk per reported source case decreased by 4.6% per week (95% CI: 1.7–7.4%; p = 0.0097), and was substantially lower after the epidemic peak (0.46; 95% CI: 0.17–0.73 exported cases per 1,000 source cases) than before it (1.7; 95% CI: 0.95–2.7 exported cases per 1,000 source cases).[253]

Methods¶

Historical case review and crude risk calculation: The authors identified and analysed all known Ebola disease cases outside Africa to contextualise intercontinental spread, using manual and AI-assisted internet searches of scientific articles, public health bulletins, and news reports.[254] As a crude risk measure, they calculated the number of cases occurring outside Africa per 1,000 reported cases in the originating epidemic for outbreaks since 2000, producing separate estimates including and excluding medically evacuated cases.[255] This calculation applies globally across all destination countries outside Africa combined, with a UK-specific example provided at the national level.

Time-varying Poisson regression: For cases exported from the 2014–16 West African epidemic specifically, the authors conducted a time-varying risk analysis using Poisson regression of the weekly count of exported cases by country (Guinea, Liberia, Sierra Leone) against the number of cases reported that week and a linear time trend.[256] This produced an estimate of how exported case counts scaled with source-country incidence, with no significant deviation from linear scaling found.[257] This method applies to the 2014–16 epidemic as a historical comparator and is not applied directly to the 2026 BVD outbreak.

Assumptions¶

Linear scaling of exportation risk with source incidence: The Poisson regression assumes that exported case counts scale linearly with weekly source-country incidence, an assumption the analysis found consistent with the 2014–16 data (estimated scaling: 0.98; 95% CI: 0.60–1.36; p = 0.86).[257] This assumption underpins the use of crude per-1,000-cases risk ratios to project forward.

Crude risk ratio as applicable across outbreaks: The crude risk measure is calculated for Ebola disease outbreaks since the year 2000[258], implicitly assuming that the historical ratio of exported-to-source cases is informative for the current 2026 BVD outbreak. The analysis does not explicitly state whether this assumption holds given differences in travel patterns, response capacity, or the specific virus species involved.

Distinction between medically evacuated and latent cases: The analysis distinguishes between medically evacuated cases, who represent known risks manageable with strict biosecurity, and latent cases arriving via commercial flights who need to be diagnosed before isolation.[259] The risk ratios are computed separately for these two categories, with the latent-only figure treated as the more policy-relevant measure of uncontrolled importation risk.

Completeness of historical case ascertainment: Agreement among search methods and with a prior clinical review supports that the case list is likely complete, but potential under-ascertainment may arise from underrepresentation of public health bulletins not published in English, and from possible geographic biases in web-based search tools.[260]

Limitations¶

Potential under-ascertainment of exported cases: Public health bulletins not published in English were underrepresented despite targeted native-language searches, and web-based search tools may have returned geographically biased results, potentially leading to missed cases.[261]

Historical data may not reflect current outbreak conditions: The analysis applies risk ratios derived primarily from the 2014–16 West African epidemic — a very large, prolonged epidemic with substantial international humanitarian response — to the 2026 BVD context. The authors note that the decrease in exportation risk over the 2014–16 epidemic is likely most consistent with improvement in infection prevention and control measures coinciding with the stepping-up of response efforts in late 2014[262], but do not formally assess whether this dynamic applies to the current, structurally different outbreak.

Per-country risks not estimated: The crude risk estimate represents the empirically observed risk across all countries outside Africa; per-country risks are smaller[263], and the analysis provides only the aggregate outside-Africa figure plus a single UK example, without ranking or estimating risk for individual destination countries.

Latent case detection depends on screening: The four latent cases identified occurred among 300,000 screened travellers and were all asymptomatic at exit and entry screening; their eventual identification relied on screening protocols that included guidance on self-monitoring of symptoms.[264] The analysis does not assess what the latent case rate would have been in the absence of such protocols.

Data sources¶

Primary outbreak data: The analysis identified 28 confirmed Ebola disease cases outside Africa in total, of which 27 occurred during the 2014–16 West African epidemic and one has occurred so far during the 2026 BVD epidemic.[265] Sources consulted included 27 scientific articles, 9 public health bulletins, 82 news articles, and 9 other sites.[266]

Auxiliary data: Weekly case counts for Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone (total 28,592 confirmed, probable, and suspected cases) covering the 2014–16 epidemic were used as the source-country incidence series in the Poisson regression.[267] The authors also reference the approximately 300,000 travellers screened by CDC-supported exit screening programmes in Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone during the 2014–16 epidemic, as context for the four identified latent cases.[268] Findings were checked against existing lists of Ebola disease cases.[269]

References¶

  1. Bouhentala, O.W. and Kadir, M.Y. (2026) ‘Epidemiological analysis of the 2026 Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak and rapid risk assessment for North Africa and Europe’, medRxiv [Preprint]. doi: 10.64898/2026.07.19.26358411.

  2. Chamla, D., Belizaire, M.R.D., Co, I.F., Jinadu, A., Mamadu, I. and Atagbaza, A.O. (2026) ‘Size of the 2026 Ebola outbreak and risk of cross-border spillover from Bundibugyo virus in Ituri Province, DR Congo, and its implications for preparedness: a recalibrated stochastic modelling study’, The Lancet Infectious Diseases. Online first. doi: 10.1016/S1473-3099(26)00320-8.

  3. Epistorm (2026). Ebola Bundibugyo Virus — DRC · Spread Risk Assessment #4 · International Dissemination. Available at: https://epistorm.github.io/EBV2026/report_04_alt.html (Accessed 8 July 2026).

  4. Fanelli, F., Parino, F., Poletto, C. and Colizza, V. (2026) ‘Shifting patterns of importation risk of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease to Europe under outbreak expansion scenarios’, medRxiv [Preprint]. doi: 10.64898/2026.05.31.26354511.

  5. Gashema, P. and Muvunyi, C.M. (2026) ‘Can stochastic modelling predict cross-border spread of the Bundibugyo Ebola virus outbreak in Africa?’, The Lancet Infectious Diseases. Online first. doi: 10.1016/S1473-3099(26)00352-X.

  6. Kinoshita, R., Suzuki, M. and Yoneoka, D. (2026) ‘Early assessment of potential airline-mediated importation risk during the 2026 DRC-Uganda Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak’, medRxiv [Preprint]. doi: 10.64898/2026.06.01.26354569.

  7. Okingo, R.L., Iroungou, B.A. and Valdano, E. (2026) ‘Introduction and sustained-transmission risk across DRC health zones during the Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak’, medRxiv [Preprint]. doi: 10.64898/2026.06.11.26355237.

  8. Richard, D.M., Routledge, I., Koeller, S. et al. (2026) ‘Assessment of risk to the U.S. population from the Ebola disease outbreak caused by Bundibugyo virus, 2026’, MMWR Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report, 75, pp. 290–292. doi: 10.15585/mmwr.mm7522e2.

  9. van Zandvoort, K., Procter, S.R., Azam, J.M., Sherratt, K. and Davies, N.G. (2026) ‘The risk of global Ebola virus spread is low: epidemiology of Ebola disease cases outside Africa, 1976 to May 2026’, Eurosurveillance, 31(24), 2600508. doi: 10.2807/1560-7917.ES.2026.31.24.2600508.

  10. Walekhwa, A.W., Mbaka, P., Nannyonga, B.K., Silal, S.P., Bbuye, M., Nakiire, L., Lamorde, M., Suilamon, T.A., Kwesiga, B., Sabiiti, W., Turinawe, G., Nakazibwe, B., Kayiwa, J., Nantongo, M., Ipola, P.A., Kungu, P., Ario, A.R., Kagirita, A., Lubwama, B., Kirenga, B.J., Muruta, A.N., Olaro, C. and Atwine, D. (2026) ‘Network-based modelling of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease importation and spread in Uganda using Displacement Tracking Matrix flow data and non-pharmaceutical intervention compliance scenarios’, medRxiv [Preprint]. doi: 10.64898/2026.06.22.26356210.

Methods overview

These pages are produced by a semi-automated evidence-synthesis pipeline that pairs AI models with human review at each major checkpoint: one model reads each source analysis on its own and grounds every extracted claim in a verbatim quoted passage; a second model takes the resulting structured comparison as input, and drafts the narrative. Human analysts then review and edit the output to ensure accuracy and completeness prior to publishing.

Identifying analyses. Analyses are identified through continuous monitoring of preprint servers, peer-reviewed publications, and grey literature. An analysis is included if it directly addresses the 2026 Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) outbreak and provides a quantitative estimate relevant to a given topic (e.g., outbreak size, risk of spread). Each included source is logged, and if the authors later release an updated version, the newer version supersedes the earlier one.

Preparing the source text. Each source text, regardless of source type (e.g., PDF, HTML), is converted to Markdown. A human analyst then curates the converted file and removes sections that carry no evidentiary content, such as references, acknowledgments, author contributions, affiliations, and declarations of interest, to keep extraction focused and reduce the number of input tokens used.

Extracting structured evidence. The curated text is split into individually citeable units, one per sentence, and passed to Claude Sonnet 4.6. For each of a fixed set of dimensions (e.g., estimates, methods, limitations), the model answers from that one source alone and must ground every factual claim in a verbatim span of its text.

Synthesizing the narrative. OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 model reads the dimensions extracted for each analysis and synthesizes the information, covering the state of the evidence, how the estimates compare and why they diverge, the key uncertainties and assumptions shared across analyses, what would most reduce that uncertainty, and what it implies for the response.

Human review and publication. Human analysts edit the draft for accuracy, completeness, and tone prior to publishing.

Limitations. This pipeline surfaces only publicly available analyses; works that have not been made public are not captured.

Footnotes¶

  1. World Health Organization (2026) “Disease Outbreak News, 17 July 2026”: “Since the previous Disease Outbreak News was published on 3 July 2026, the number of confirmed cases and deaths has increased substantially in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. In total, 2145 confirmed cases have been reported: 2124 in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (including two cases with diagnosis in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and subsequent treatment in Germany), 20 in Uganda and one in France.”

    ↩

  2. Chamla et al. (2026) “Summary”: “Cross-border spillover remained material: Uganda 94.2% importation probability (19 confirmed cases as of 4 June 2026, including five healthcare-worker infections and two deaths); South Sudan 69.3%; Rwanda 8.6%; and Burundi 2.0%.”

    ↩↩↩

  3. Gashema & Muvunyi (2026) “Considering epidemiological and contextual factors, the study estimated a high probability of regional spillover to Uganda (94·2%) and South Sudan (69·3%), identifying these countries as priority areas for preparedness. By contrast, the projected risks for Rwanda (8·6%) and Burundi (2·0%) remain comparatively low.”

    ↩↩↩

  4. Epistorm (2026) “Executive Summary”: “** The two immediate regional neighbours are removed from the destination set; their cross-border mobility is characterised as regional spillover, separate from the international dissemination question this report addresses. After renormalisation, conditional risk concentrates in the next regional ring (South Sudan, Tanzania, Kenya) and an intercontinental tail led by the UAE.”

    ↩

  5. Kinoshita, Suzuki, & Yoneoka (2026) “Abstract”: “Effective-distance analyses identified major international hub countries, including Belgium, France, South Africa, Kenya, and the United Arab Emirates, as higher-probability gateways until the end of June, 2026.”

    ↩↩

  6. Fanelli et al. 2026 “Abstract”: “Across all scenarios, France, Italy, and the United Kingdom remained among the most exposed countries.”

    ↩↩

  7. Chamla et al. (2026) “Methods”: “These parameters were not empirically derived for this outbreak and represent the principal sources of uncertainty in spillover projections.”

    ↩↩↩

  8. Walekhwa et al. (2026) “4. Discussion”: “The DTM data cover only a 10-day window and eight border crossing points; movements through unofficial crossings or after the outbreak escalated may not be captured, a limitation common to mobility-based epidemiological studies. While we imputed annual estimates and used time-dependent decay, real mobility patterns can change unpredictably.”

    ↩↩↩

  9. Luvande, Iroungou, & Valdano (2026) “Body”: “These results separate importation from amplification risk: health zones near the affected area are more likely to receive cases, but not necessarily to sustain transmission. Our estimates therefore differ from Ref. 5 by identifying areas of concern farther from the affected zone.”

    ↩

  10. Kinoshita, Suzuki, & Yoneoka (2026) “Airline-flow-based network and effective distance for hazard estimation”: “Estimated arrival probability should be interpreted as importation risk rather than the risk of local transmission after arrival. Importation does not necessarily lead to onward spread because EBOD requires direct contact with infectious body fluids or contaminated materials, and timely detection, isolation, contact tracing and infection prevention and control can reduce transmission.”

    ↩↩

  11. Chamla et al. (2026) “Discussion”: “Future iterations should integrate line lists, contact-tracing data and dynamic mobility estimates.”

    ↩↩

  12. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “4. Discussion”: “Reporting dates do not represent symptom onset, and the analysis could not estimate time-varying reproduction numbers, serial intervals, onset-to-isolation delays, household secondary-attack rates, health-zone-specific growth, or intervention effects.”

    ↩↩

  13. Walekhwa et al. (2026) “4. Discussion”: “Real-time updating, running the model weekly with new DTM data and case counts, would transform this retrospective analysis into a prospective decision support tool, as advocated by Kraemer et al. (2018) for real-time epidemic response.”

    ↩↩

  14. Chamla et al. (2026) “Discussion”: “Although South Sudan’s importation probability is lower than Uganda’s, documented deficits across all five WHO preparedness domains mean any importation would generate a substantially larger secondary outbreak. Closing these gaps within 4 to 8 weeks is the principal operational window.”

    ↩↩

  15. Walekhwa et al. (2026) “4. Discussion”: “The practical implication is that early outbreak response should include rapid estimation of the infectious period and serial interval for the specific Ebola strain. Investing in field studies to characterise these biological parameters, through contact tracing data and serial interval analysis, could yield greater returns than refining operational protocols that are already performing adequately, a strategy advocated by Chowell et al. (2019) for Ebola preparedness.”

    ↩

  16. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “4. Discussion”: “This pattern shows that cross-border importation and healthcare-associated secondary transmission can occur without progression to sustained community transmission when surveillance, isolation, contact management, and specialised care function effectively.”

    ↩

  17. Luvande, Iroungou, & Valdano (2026) “Body”: “Our results warrant preparedness in health zones with high sustained-transmission potential but lower introduction risk, particularly in Kinshasa and other urban centers, despite their distance from the outbreak. This is relevant because several high-amplification areas are in Kinshasa, Kasaï, and Tshopo, where preparedness may be more feasible than in parts of eastern DRC despite heterogeneous healthcare access, accessibility constraints, and low-to-moderate insecurity outside Kinshasa.”

    ↩

  18. van Zandvoort et al. (2026) “Discussion”: “Crucially, most Ebola disease cases reported outside Africa (27 of 28 cases) have been either (i) primary cases in individuals with a known responsive or occupational exposure to an Ebola disease epidemic (24 cases, including 20 healthcare workers, three UN employees and one journalist, among whom 20 were medically evacuated) or (ii) secondary cases in healthcare workers treating an Ebola disease patient outside Africa (3 cases). There was only one reported confirmed case outside Africa in a traveller with no responsive or occupational exposure.”

    ↩

  19. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “Abstract”: “Preparedness in North Africa and Europe should prioritise complete exposure histories, rapid isolation, validated diagnostics, protected clinical care, and contact management rather than reliance on border screening.”

    ↩

    ↩

  21. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “Abstract”: “Introduction likelihood was assessed as very low to low for North Africa and very low for the general European population; delayed recognition in routine healthcare was the principal scenario for limited secondary transmission.”

The July-to-June notification-rate ratio was 1.32 (counting-model 95% interval 1.20–1.46).”

    ↩

  24. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “3. Results”: “ECDC estimated approximately one importation per 23,000–24,000 travellers from the principal outbreak region, with a 90% uncertainty interval of one per 13,000–54,000.     ↩

  25. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “2. Methods”: “This was a rapid public-health analysis of publicly available aggregate surveillance data and official risk assessments.     ↩

  26. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “2. Methods”: “Airline sources were used only to establish the existence of plausible routes and not to estimate passenger volumes.”

    ↩↩

  27. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “2. Methods”: “Cumulative confirmed DRC counts were indexed by reporting date rather than symptom-onset date. Exact 95% Poisson counting intervals were calculated using the Garwood method.”

    ↩

    ↩

  29. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “2. Methods”: “No reproduction number was estimated because onset-date line-list data were unavailable.”

    ↩

  30. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “2. Methods”: “Risk was separated into four dimensions: introduction likelihood, conditional onward-transmission likelihood after an importation, impact if detection was delayed, and confidence in the judgement. Categories were comparative reference classes rather than calibrated probabilities. Boundary ratings such as ‘very low to low’ were retained when the evidence did not support a more precise classification.”

    ↩

  31. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “2. Methods”: “The ratings were calibrated against WHO’s 6 June assessment, which classified risk as very high in the DRC, high in Uganda and bordering countries, low in the remainder of the WHO African Region, and low globally.”

    ↩

  32. Bouhentala & Kadir (2026) “2. Methods”: “These intervals describe counting variation only and do not incorporate systematic uncertainty from backlogs, under-ascertainment, changing testing, or reporting delays.”

    ↩
